---
title: "Importance-sampling EM in nlmixr2 (imp / impmap / qrpem) vs NONMEM and Phoenix, FOCEI and SAEM"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    self_contained: no
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Importance-sampling EM in nlmixr2 (imp / impmap / qrpem) vs NONMEM and Phoenix, FOCEI and SAEM}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, echo=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  out.width = "100%"
  )
library(nlmixr2save)
## this article lives in vignettes/articles/, so the shared cache and logo one
## directory up (vignettes/) are referenced with "../".  The `:=` operator
## (from nlmixr2save) runs each fit once and caches it under the prefix below, so
## the article renders with live output without refitting on every build.
options(nlmixr2save.dir = "../cache",
        nlmixr2save.prefix = "imp-",
        nlmixr2save.check = FALSE)
```

![nlmixr](../logo.png)

# The importance-sampling EM family (`imp`, `impmap`, `qrpem`)

`nlmixr2` provides three closely related **Monte-Carlo expectation-maximization**
estimators, all built on one importance-sampling kernel and differing only in how
the per-subject E-step is sampled:

* `est = "imp"` -- importance sampling with the proposal centered at each
  subject's running conditional mean, over-dispersed (`gamma * Omega`) for
  robustness. This is **NONMEM's `METHOD=IMP`**.
* `est = "impmap"` -- importance sampling with the proposal centered at each
  subject's **MAP mode** with the inner (conditional) Hessian as covariance. This
  is **NONMEM's `METHOD=IMPMAP`**.
* `est = "qrpem"` -- the same EM but with **Sobol quasi-random** importance
  samples and a SIR-accelerated M-step. This is the **quasi-random parametric EM**
  of Leary & Dunlavey (2012) -- the method **Phoenix NLME** made its flagship.

What unites them, and separates them from FOCEI, is that they estimate the
**marginal likelihood by Monte-Carlo integration** rather than by a Laplace
approximation. The population parameters then come out of a standard EM: an
importance-sampling E-step that computes each subject's expected sufficient
statistics (and its marginal likelihood), and an M-step that updates the typical
values, `Omega`, and residual error.

This article leads with a worked example; the algorithm and a comparison with
NONMEM, Phoenix, FOCEI and SAEM come at the end.

# Worked example: the same answer, an exact likelihood, and less noise

We use the theophylline data (`theo_sd`) and a one-compartment oral model with
between-subject variability on `ka`, `cl` and `v`.

```{r model}
library(nlmixr2)

impModel <- function() {
  ini({
    tka <- 0.45; tcl <- 1; tv <- 3.45
    eta.ka ~ 0.6; eta.cl ~ 0.3; eta.v ~ 0.1
    add.sd <- 0.7
  })
  model({
    ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)
    cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)
    v  <- exp(tv  + eta.v)
    d/dt(depot)  <- -ka * depot
    d/dt(center) <-  ka * depot - cl / v * center
    cp <- center / v
    cp ~ add(add.sd)
  })
}
```

## They agree with FOCEI and SAEM -- but the objective is an exact likelihood

Only `est =` changes across all five methods:

```{r fits}
fitFocei  := nlmixr2(impModel, nlmixr2data::theo_sd, est = "focei",
                     control = foceiControl(print = 0L))
fitSaem   := nlmixr2(impModel, nlmixr2data::theo_sd, est = "saem",
                     control = saemControl(nBurn = 200L, nEm = 300L, print = 0L))
fitImp    := nlmixr2(impModel, nlmixr2data::theo_sd, est = "imp",
                     control = impControl(print = 0L))
fitImpmap := nlmixr2(impModel, nlmixr2data::theo_sd, est = "impmap",
                     control = impmapControl(print = 0L))
fitQrpem  := nlmixr2(impModel, nlmixr2data::theo_sd, est = "qrpem",
                     control = qrpemControl(print = 0L))
```

```{r compare}
round(rbind(FOCEI  = fitFocei$theta,  SAEM   = fitSaem$theta,
            imp    = fitImp$theta,    impmap = fitImpmap$theta,
            qrpem  = fitQrpem$theta), 3)
## objective function (-2LL)
round(c(FOCEI = fitFocei$objf, SAEM = fitSaem$objf, imp = fitImp$objf,
        impmap = fitImpmap$objf, qrpem = fitQrpem$objf), 2)
```

All five recover the same parameters. Look at the objective values: FOCEI and the
three importance-sampling fits agree to about `0.03` here -- because theophylline
is nearly linear, so FOCEI's Laplace approximation to the marginal likelihood is
accurate. The point is *what the number is*: FOCEI's objective is the **Laplace
approximation** of the `-2LL`, while the `imp`/`impmap`/`qrpem` objective is a
**Monte-Carlo estimate of the true marginal `-2LL`** -- the same quantity NONMEM's
`IMP`/`IMPMAP` report, with the same constant convention, so it is directly
comparable to FOCEI. When the Laplace approximation is *not* accurate (strongly
nonlinear PK/PD, sparse data, or categorical/count/ordinal endpoints), the two
numbers diverge and the importance-sampling likelihood is the trustworthy one.

(SAEM's reported objective uses a separate likelihood evaluation and a different
constant, so it is not on the same footing -- compare SAEM to SAEM.)

## Why quasi-random (QRPEM): less Monte-Carlo noise for the same cost

A Monte-Carlo E-step has Monte-Carlo error: with a finite number of importance
samples, the converged estimate wobbles from one random seed to the next. This is
the problem QRPEM solves. Pseudo-random samples fill the parameter space
unevenly, so the integration error shrinks only as `1 / sqrt(N)`; **Sobol
low-discrepancy (quasi-random) samples** fill it evenly, shrinking the error much
faster. The payoff: for the *same* (small) number of importance samples, `qrpem`
gives a far more reproducible objective than pseudo-random `imp`.

Fit both at a deliberately small `isample = 30` across several seeds and compare
the spread of the converged objective:

```{r seeds, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
library(ggplot2)

seedStudy := local({
  seeds <- 1:5
  do.call(rbind, lapply(c("imp", "qrpem"), function(m) {
    objf <- vapply(seeds, function(s) {
      ctl <- if (m == "imp")
        impControl(print = 0L, isample = 30L, nIter = 80L, impSeed = s)
      else
        qrpemControl(print = 0L, isample = 30L, nIter = 80L, impSeed = s)
      suppressWarnings(as.numeric(nlmixr2(impModel, nlmixr2data::theo_sd,
                                          est = m, control = ctl)$objf))
    }, numeric(1))
    data.frame(method = m, seed = seeds, objf = objf)
  }))
})

## seed-to-seed spread of the converged -2LL
aggregate(objf ~ method, seedStudy, function(x) c(sd = sd(x), range = diff(range(x))))

ggplot(seedStudy, aes(method, objf, colour = method)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.08, height = 0, size = 2.5) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "crossbar", width = 0.35, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "converged -2LL (5 seeds, isample = 30)",
       title = "Quasi-random (qrpem) converges to a tighter objective than pseudo-random (imp)") +
  theme_bw() + theme(legend.position = "none")
```

The `qrpem` objectives cluster tightly; the `imp` objectives scatter several-fold
wider for the *same* number of samples. In practice that means `qrpem` reaches a
given precision with fewer importance samples -- the reason it is a popular
production EM method. (At the default `isample = 300` both are already tight;
quasi-random simply lets you use far fewer.)

# When to choose the importance-sampling EM family

Reach for `imp` / `impmap` / `qrpem` when:

* you need a **trustworthy objective function** for model comparison and you do
  not trust FOCEI's Laplace approximation -- strongly nonlinear models, sparse
  data, or non-normal (categorical, count, ordered-categorical, time-to-event)
  endpoints, where the marginal likelihood is genuinely non-Gaussian;
* you want a fit that is **numerically comparable to NONMEM `IMP`/`IMPMAP`** or to
  **Phoenix QRPEM**, e.g. to reproduce or cross-check a regulatory submission run;
* you want **importance-sampling standard errors** (`covMethod = "imp"`, the
  default) computed from the same Monte-Carlo likelihood rather than a Laplace or
  sandwich estimator;
* (`qrpem`) you want the **lowest Monte-Carlo noise per sample** -- the
  quasi-random E-step is the most sample-efficient of the three.

Use `impmap` when a good MAP-centered proposal is available (it usually needs
fewer samples than `imp`); use `imp` when the MAP search is unstable and the
robust over-dispersed proposal is safer. Stay with **FOCEI** when the model is
close to linear and you want the fastest deterministic fit; use **SAEM** when the
likelihood is difficult or multimodal and a robust MCMC E-step is worth the cost
(SAEM and the IMP family are complementary -- both are EM, differing in how the
E-step is done).

---

The remainder of this article is the algorithm reference: how importance-sampling
EM works, how the three proposals differ, and how it maps onto NONMEM, Phoenix,
FOCEI and SAEM. Skip it unless you want the internals.

# How importance-sampling EM works

The marginal likelihood of subject `i` integrates the individual random effects
out of the joint model:

```
p(y_i) = integral  p(y_i | eta) p(eta) d(eta)
```

FOCEI approximates this integral by a Laplace expansion around the mode -- fast,
deterministic, but biased when the integrand is not close to Gaussian. EM instead
maximizes the likelihood by iterating an **E-step** (expected complete-data
log-likelihood given the current parameters) and an **M-step** (maximize it). For
NLME the E-step needs each subject's posterior expectations, and the
importance-sampling family computes them by Monte-Carlo:

1. **Proposal.** Pick an easy-to-sample Gaussian `q_i(eta)` close to the
   posterior `p(eta | y_i)` (the choice is what distinguishes the three methods).
2. **Sample and weight.** Draw `isample` points `eta^(s) ~ q_i`, and weight each
   by the importance ratio `w^(s) = p(y_i | eta^(s)) p(eta^(s)) / q_i(eta^(s))`.
3. **E-step estimates.** The weighted samples give an (unbiased) Monte-Carlo
   estimate of the marginal likelihood `p(y_i)` *and* of the posterior moments
   `E[eta_i | y_i]`, `E[eta_i eta_i^T | y_i]` -- the sufficient statistics.
4. **M-step.** Update the population parameters from the pooled sufficient
   statistics: the typical values (through the mu-referenced linear model),
   `Omega` as the mean posterior second moment, and the residual-error terms.

Repeat to convergence. Because the samples are independent, the E-step is
**embarrassingly parallel** across subjects and samples, and the marginal
likelihood falls out every iteration for free.

```{r impCycle, echo=FALSE}
DiagrammeR::mermaid("
graph TD
  P[\"Build the per-subject proposal q_i (imp: conditional mean; impmap: MAP mode + Hessian)\"] --> S[\"Draw isample importance points (imp/impmap: pseudo-random; qrpem: Sobol quasi-random)\"]
  S --> W[\"Weight by the importance ratio p(y|eta)p(eta)/q(eta)\"]
  W --> E[\"E-step: Monte-Carlo marginal likelihood and posterior moments\"]
  E --> M[\"M-step: update typical values, Omega, residual error (qrpem: SIR-accelerated)\"]
  M --> C{\"Converged?\"}
  C -- no --> P
  C -- yes --> O[\"Population estimates + importance-sampling -2LL and covariance\"]
")
```

## What the three proposals change

* **`imp` (NONMEM `METHOD=IMP`).** The proposal is centered at the subject's
  running conditional mean with covariance `gamma * Omega` -- deliberately
  *over-dispersed* so the importance weights stay well behaved even when the
  proposal is off. No per-iteration mode search, so each iteration is cheap and
  robust, at the cost of needing more samples.
* **`impmap` (NONMEM `METHOD=IMPMAP`).** Each iteration first finds the subject's
  MAP mode and uses the inner conditional Hessian as the proposal covariance -- a
  much tighter fit to the posterior, so fewer samples are needed, provided the
  mode search is stable.
* **`qrpem` (Phoenix-style QRPEM).** Replaces the pseudo-random draws with a
  **Sobol quasi-random** sequence (lower integration error per sample) and adds a
  **SIR (sampling-importance-resampling)** acceleration of the non-mu / residual
  M-step. `qrpemControl()` is exactly `impmapControl(qr = TRUE, sir = TRUE)`.

The number of importance samples (`isample`, default 300), the proposal
over-dispersion (`gamma`), and the importance-sampling covariance
(`covMethod = "imp"`) are shared knobs across all three.

# How it maps onto other tools

**NONMEM.** `imp` and `impmap` are nlmixr2's implementations of `METHOD=IMP` and
`METHOD=IMPMAP`: the same importance-sampling EM, the same over-dispersed vs
MAP-centered proposal distinction, and the same Monte-Carlo `-2LL` (`covMethod =
"imp"` mirrors NONMEM's importance-sampling covariance step). This makes an
nlmixr2 `imp`/`impmap` objective directly comparable to a NONMEM `IMP`/`IMPMAP`
one.

**Phoenix NLME (Certara).** QRPEM is Phoenix's flagship EM method, from Leary &
Dunlavey. nlmixr2's `qrpem` implements the **published** quasi-random parametric
EM with SIR acceleration; Phoenix's production code is proprietary and its exact
details are not public, so the two are not guaranteed identical, but they share
the defining ideas (Sobol importance samples, SIR-accelerated convergence).

**FOCEI.** FOCEI is a *deterministic Laplace approximation* of the marginal
likelihood; the IMP family is a *Monte-Carlo evaluation* of the same likelihood.
They agree when the posterior is near-Gaussian and diverge when it is not -- which
is precisely when you want the importance-sampling number. FOCEI is faster and
noise-free; the IMP family is (asymptotically) unbiased.

**SAEM.** SAEM is also an EM, but its E-step is an **MCMC** chain with
stochastic-approximation (Robbins-Monro) averaging across iterations, and it does
not produce the marginal likelihood directly (a separate importance-sampling or
quadrature step is needed for the `-2LL`). The IMP family's E-step uses
**independent importance samples** and yields the likelihood every iteration.
SAEM is exceptionally robust for difficult or multimodal problems; the IMP family
is efficient and self-certifying on the likelihood when a good proposal exists.

| Aspect | FOCEI | SAEM | **imp / impmap / qrpem** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Marginal likelihood | Laplace approximation | Not direct (separate step) | **Monte-Carlo importance sampling (near-exact)** |
| E-step | Analytic conditional step | MCMC + stochastic approximation | Importance sampling (independent draws) |
| Objective for model comparison | Laplace `-2LL` | Own convention | **Exact `-2LL`; matches NONMEM IMP** |
| Character | Deterministic, fast | Stochastic, very robust | Stochastic, unbiased, parallel |
| Non-normal / nonlinear posteriors | Biased | Handles well | **Handles well** |
| External equivalents | NONMEM FOCEI; Phoenix FOCE | NONMEM SAEM; Monolix | NONMEM IMP/IMPMAP; **Phoenix QRPEM** |
| Sampling efficiency (this family) | -- | -- | `qrpem` (quasi-random) > `impmap` > `imp` |

The short version: `imp`/`impmap`/`qrpem` bring NONMEM's importance-sampling EM
and Phoenix's QRPEM into nlmixr2, as a Monte-Carlo *exact-likelihood* complement
to FOCEI's Laplace approximation and SAEM's MCMC EM -- most valuable when you need
an objective you can trust for a model FOCEI's Laplace step cannot approximate
well, or a fit that lines up numerically with a NONMEM or Phoenix run.

# References

* Leary R, Dunlavey M. *QRPEM -- A Quasi-Random Parametric EM Method.* PAGE 2012;
  and Certara Phoenix NLME documentation.
* Bauer RJ. *NONMEM Tutorial Part II: Estimation Methods and Advanced Examples.*
  CPT: Pharmacometrics & Systems Pharmacology, 2019 (IMP / IMPMAP).
* Kuhn E, Lavielle M. *Maximum likelihood estimation in nonlinear mixed effects
  models.* Computational Statistics & Data Analysis, 2005 (SAEM).
* Wang Y. *Derivation of various NONMEM estimation methods.* J. Pharmacokinet.
  Pharmacodyn., 2007 (FOCE/Laplace vs the exact likelihood).
